Storing event in flower has several limitations:

1.  Events are not necessarily contain full task information. Celery truncates events if they exceed some threshold.
1. It doesn't scale well. Flower will not be able to handle events from all workers and will be a limit to  horizontal scalability.
1. The current task search implementation is not optimal and has O(n) complexity. It will not work well with big task sets.

As a documentation note, lets warn users that
"Non-Specific Exception Handling In a Call Tree Where Cancellations/Timeouts Occur"
means asking for untrackable problems. An innocent looking try/catch block 
at an unnoticable corner of a file such as

try:
    await timeout_after(10, co)
except:
    pass

will eat up your cancellations/timeouts...

Each try catch block in such a tree and "With some awaits in it",
should be ready to handle exception coming from outside AND
re-raise them.

This is something we dont have to think for sync code.

I realize some things:
1. Universal API is impractical, it's heavy depends on API authors.
2. Interoperable API is important and it's the goal of this proposal, otherwise we lost a lot of options and make a lot of incompatible libraries.
3. Replaceable Kernel(Runtime) is valuable. Improvements are put forward all the time and replaceable kernel enable painless change kernel.

I change the architecture as follows:
```
               +-------------------------------------------+
               |                                           |
               |                 application               |
               |                                           |
        +--->  +-----------+-----------                    |
        |      |           |          |                    |
  api   |      | curio-api | trio-api |  asyncio-libraries |
        |      |           |          |                    |
        +--->  +-----------+----------+------+             |
        |      |                             |             |
syscall |      |           syscall           |             |
        |      |                             |             |
        +--->  +--------------+--------------+             |
        |      |              |              |             |
kernel  |      | curio-kernel | trio-kernel  |             |
        |      |              |              |             |
        +--->  +--------------+--------------+-------------+
               |                                           |
               |               asyncio & loop              |
               |                                           |
               +-------------------------------------------+
```

1. I give up the idea of `stdlib`, which will also break backward compatibility of some libraries.
2. Build curio & trio api on syscall, no direct depends on kernel, so that different apis are interoperable. 
3. Implement syscall in curio & trio kernel.
4. Implement syscall in curio & trio kernel over asyncio, so that curio/trio/asyncio-libraries are interoperable. (make kernel a wrapper of asyncio)

To achieve the goals, the syscall should meet requirements below:
1. Support to build curio & trio api without break (maybe most of) backward compatibility.
2. Keep small and primitive.
3. Can be implement.
4. Can be implement on the top of asyncio.

I think (1)(2)(3) is possible and not too difficulty, (4) is hard but also possible.

Hi,

thank you for letting me know what's happening! Unfortunately, I don't think I'll make you happy with my answer.

TOML is a bad file format. It looks good at first glance, and for really really trivial things it is probably good. But once I started using it and the configuration schema became more complex, I found the syntax ugly and hard to read.

I personally abandoned TOML and as such, I'm not planning on adding any new features (there are in fact issues open due to changes in the datetime format, which btw TOML people made without bumping the specs version number, so there are probably incompatible implementations of 0.4.0 out there. In any case, I'm not planning on working on those issues).

That being said, I do accept merge requests and I do fix bugs (that are not due to specs change). The support for 2.6 and 3.3 is trivial and I don't foresee it as a maintenance problem. I'm willing to fix the error message issue if you decide to adopt TOML (please don't). But that's pretty much all you can expect of me. I'd be happy to release the toml name on pypi if someone else wants to fork and continue this project.

If you're still reading, I'd like to put forth CSON as a format of choice. I happen to have a Python implementation too: https://github.com/avakar/pycson. It's missing in your comparison but I think it scores "yes" in all rows of your summary table (except maybe "well-defined", but that's a stretch for TOML too). It's quite popular in the Javascript world so there is some tooling for it available.

OK so you're dealing with two different sets of credentials here.

1. AWS credentials. When running on an EC2 instance, your application (reflow) should get its AWS credentials from EC2 instance metadata. I know Reflow is capable of ingesting these, but you may need to configure it properly to do so. I think @ryanking mentioned something in Slack about how Reflow fixed something about this recently. (Also, you need to launch the instance with an IAM role - if you don't specify an IAM role, the instance won't have AWS credentials in its metadata. But if you're launching with aegea, your instance should always have an IAM role.)

2. SSH credentials. What is reflow trying to use the credentials for? I'm not certain that they're required, but if you're sure they are, then my guess is that you'd want to use the AWS credentials manager using the AWS credentials in step 1. Something like: `cat ~/.ssh/id_rsa.pub | aegea secrets put reflow-ssh-pubkey` `aws secretsmanager get-secret-value --secret-id reflow-ssh-pubkey > ~/.ssh/id_rsa.pub`.

Should work now (as of #184 , can be installed via `pip install git+git://github.com/rasbt/mlxtend.git`). Here's an example:

```python
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from mlxtend.plotting import plot_decision_regions

from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.naive_bayes import GaussianNB 
from sklearn import datasets
import numpy as np

# Loading some example data
iris = datasets.load_iris()
X = iris.data[:, 2]
X = X[:, None]
y = iris.target

# Initializing and fitting classifiers
clf1 = LogisticRegression(random_state=1)
clf2 = GaussianNB()
clf1.fit(X, y)
clf2.fit(X, y)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(10, 3))

fig = plot_decision_regions(X=X, y=y, clf=clf1, ax=axes[0], legend=2)
fig = plot_decision_regions(X=X, y=y, clf=clf2, ax=axes[1], legend=1)
    
plt.show()
```

[test.py](https://gist.githubusercontent.com/whalebot-helmsman/28b875cc0edb2bd6f69bffbad3a02162/raw/e5102927217900bc730e91d2217ed17bdf334f27/test.py) imitates hard (in a sense of CPU time required) train sets with slow classifier on a small public dataset. ```
```
time python test.py 1
time python test.py 2
time python test.py 4
time python test.py 8 
```

```
    time.sleep(1)

board.close()
```

> We can discuss this over on SciPy eventually, but I assume the way it works now is that the filters get stored somewhere for fast access like a LUT? If so, is there some way a user could precompute them themselves, and (optionally) pass them into the function?

Exactly: the coefficients are pre-computed and stored in a buffer which gets passed into the interpolator.  Because the same sets of coefficients are used so frequently (heh), we include pre-computed LUTs in npz files which can be referred to by name (eg, `kaiser_fast`, `kaiser_best`, etc).  This step isn't strictly necessary, but every little bit helps; it's pretty easy to do via pkg_resources, but I don't think there's precedent for this in scipy.  Moreover, if we in the future decide to include filters derived by numerical optimization (instead of sinc+kaiser), this would be the only way to go.

You can also provide parameters for the `filters.sinc_window` function to construct the filter coefficients on the fly, which is more in keeping with existing idioms in scipy (eg, fft window functions).

We don't provide a way for users to directly provide the LUT, since there are a couple of other parameters that need to be tracked (ie, the full output tuple from `sinc_window`).  It wouldn't be hard to expand the filter loader to allow user-supplied filters, but I'm not sure how often such a thing would get used.

Hey, I was thinking about this, it seems like probably the nicest way to get this would be to use the shared edges in face adjacency. That avoids some of the thorny edge cases with non- watertight meshes (no adjacent face, 3+ triangles sharing an edge, etc):

```
    # the shared edges that correspond to adjacent face pairs
    # IE: len(mesh.face_adjacency_edges) == len(mesh.face_adjacency)
    edges = mesh.face_adjacency_edges

    # get a unit vector along the edges
    edge_vector = np.diff(mesh.vertices[edges],axis=1).reshape((-1,3))
    edge_vector /= np.linalg.norm(edge_vector, axis=1).reshape((-1,1))

    # cross the edge vector with the first face
    vec_A = np.cross(edge_vector,
                     m.face_normals[m.face_adjacency[:,0]])

    # cross the edge vectors with the second face
    vec_B = np.cross(edge_vector,
                     m.face_normals[m.face_adjacency[:,1]])

```

```
class Import(object):
    """
    >>> Import(Use(lambda x: 'V'), Use(lambda x: 'I')).validate(1)
    'V'
    >>> Import(Use(lambda x: 'V'), Use(lambda x: 'I')).validate(1, importing=True)
    'I'
    """
    def __init__(self, validation, processing):
        self.validation = validation
        self.processing = processing

    def validate(self, data, **kwargs):
        if kwargs.get('importing', False):
            return self.processing.validate(data, **kwargs)
        return self.validation.validate(data, **kwargs)
```

The difficulty, as you've discovered, is that hotkey callbacks operate on a different thread, and Tkinter doesn't play nicely with multiprocessing.

If you're just trying to abort the script, I've found this usually works:

    import thread
    import keyboard
    def _abort_script():
        thread.interrupt_main()
    keyboard.add_hotkey("alt+shift+c", _abort_script, suppress=True)

`thread.interrupt_main()` sends an interrupt (like pressing Ctrl+C) to the main thread, which (unless you have some kind of exception handling for that) causes it to terminate immediately.

If you're trying to do something more complex, there are other ways to work around Tkinter's threading limitations.

`#!/usr/bin/env python

"""
Example from pybedtools documentation (:ref:`third example`) to count \
reads in introns and exons using multiple CPUs.
"""

from __future__ import print_function
import pybedtools
import argparse
import os
import sys
import multiprocessing


def featuretype_filter(feature, featuretype):
    """
    Only passes features with the specified *featuretype*
    """
    if feature[2] == featuretype:
        return True
    return False


def subset_featuretypes(featuretype):
    return g.filter(featuretype_filter, featuretype).saveas()


def count_reads_in_features(features):
    """
    Callback function to count reads in features
    """
    return features.intersect(abam=bam,
                             b=features.fn,
                             s=stranded,
                             bed=True,
                             stream=True).count()


def main():
    """
    Third quick example from the documentation -- count reads introns and
    exons, in parallel
    """
    ap = argparse.ArgumentParser(prog=os.path.basename(sys.argv[0]),
                                 usage=__doc__)
    ap.add_argument('--gff', required=True,
                    help='GFF or GTF file containing annotations')
    ap.add_argument('--bam', required=True,
                    help='BAM file containing reads to be counted')
    ap.add_argument('--stranded', action='store_true',
                    help='Use strand-specific merging and overlap. '
                         'Default is to ignore strand')
    ap.add_argument('--no-parallel', dest='noparallel', action='store_true',
                    help='Disables parallel computation')
    ap.add_argument('-o', '--output',
                    help='Optional file to which results will be written; '
                         'default is stdout')
    ap.add_argument('-v', '--verbose', action='store_true',
                    help='Verbose (goes to stderr)')
    args = ap.parse_args()

    gff = args.gff
    bam = args.bam
    stranded = args.stranded
    parallel = not args.noparallel

    # Some GFF files have invalid entries -- like chromosomes with negative
    # coords or features of length = 0.  This line removes them and saves the
    # result in a tempfile
    g = pybedtools.BedTool(gff).remove_invalid().saveas()

    # Decide which version of map to use.  If parallel, we only need 3
    # processes.
    pool = multiprocessing.Pool(processes=3)

    # Get separate files for introns and exons in parallel (if specified)
    featuretypes = ('intron', 'exon')
    introns, exons = pool.map(subset_featuretypes, featuretypes)

    # Perform some genome algebra to get unique and shared regions
    exon_only = exons.subtract(introns).merge().remove_invalid().saveas()
    intron_only = introns.subtract(exons).merge().remove_invalid().saveas()
    intron_and_exon = exons\
            .intersect(introns).merge().remove_invalid().saveas()

    # Do intersections with BAM file in parallel
    features = (exon_only, intron_only, intron_and_exon)
    results = pool.map(count_reads_in_features, features)

    labels = ('      exon only:',
              '    intron only:',
              'intron and exon:')

    for label, reads in zip(labels, results):
        print('%s %s' % (label, reads))

    pybedtools.cleanup(verbose=False)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

`

Ryan;
Great idea. I came on this in the context of MetaSV runs and have workarounds in place (bioinform/metasv#76) but it's probably a good idea in case folks run into it in other contexts. Thanks so much.

You're right. It's not a bit change.

IMO there are two reasons why I would call it an improvement:

1. It's now easier to do bulk calculations. Basically everything that `PySonar2` does is now easily possible: https://github.com/yinwang0/pysonar2. Before that was possible, too. However it wasn't easy to speed up, because you had to scan for names yourself and then call `jedi.goto_definitions` on it, which would flush the caches all the time. Now it should be fast and easy to use.

Oh I think I understand the race condition now (disclaimer: not tested). I think there are a number of factors, none directly related to freezegun. It looks like in running the tests, we have two threads, one to interact with the web browser, and one to handle server stuff from the client.

```
thread1 --> browser --> thread2 (http server)
```

In thread1 we login to the web browser. However we don't wait for a response. We then execute the next step, which is to freeze the datetime. At this stage the browser gets around to issuing the request to login, and the request is placed and processed in thread2 while thread1 is still busy freezing the time.

Actually, the operstate is defined in RFC2863 [1] and it has to be set up by the administrator to be read by SNMP and so on:

```python
from pyroute2 import IPRoute
with IPRoute() as ipr:
    # this will NOT bring the interface up, but MARK it as up:
    ipr.link('set', index=ipr.link_lookup(ifname='dummy0'), operstate='UP')
```

```python
from pyroute2 import IPDB
with IPDB() as ipdb:
    with ipdb.interfaces.dummy0 as i:
        i.operstate = 'UP'
```

Pls notice, that it doesn't bring the interface up, it **marks** it as up. If you will try to set the operstate == 'UP' on an interface that is really down, the `commit()` will fail.

[1] http://www.rfc-base.org/txt/rfc-2863.txt

A bit more detail here: 

It seems that the first poll() ends up resulting in:

```python
consumer.py:585:     log.debug("Node %s not ready -- failing offset fetch request"
```

Where it then loops a couple times before returning an empty offset dictionary `{}`.  refresh_committed_offsets_if_needed() then sets self._subscription.needs_fetch_committed_offsets = False without setting committed offsets on any partitions.  At which point the first poll() request returns without having set any high water marks.

This is distinct from when the topic already exits and the first call to poll() does successfully set the high water marks.

Solutions that seem to work include:
1. Increasing the timeout on the first poll() request.
2. polling multiple times before producing the first message.

But neither of those seems deterministic, instead relying on timing.

The solution that I found that seems to work is:
```
def consumerReady():
    partitions = None
    while partitions == None or len(partitions) == 0:
        partitions = consumer.partitions_for_topic(topic)
        consumer.poll(timeout)
```
and then replacing my first call to poll() with consumerReady()

Prior to deploying with that I'll add a retry count to prevent it from going into an infinite loop if it cannot connect to kafka.

Overall, it would be nice if there was a cleaner way of asking the consumer "are you ready yet?".

Hello, it's me again :) I'm back and digging into websocket support. Recently I have discovered an issue wich has similar reason.

Old one is passing `*args **kwargs` from request handler to is_authenticated method and next to verify method https://github.com/ahopkins/sanic-jwt/blob/dev/sanic_jwt/authentication.py#L225.
For Sanic websocket routes this clashing verify method argument `return_payload` with `ws` arg which sanic passes for websocket routes https://github.com/channelcat/sanic/blob/master/sanic/app.py#L300. 

#57 

And next one is a possibility to always set `verify` argument to `False` (which is very bad) in case you have a route with verify argument defined like this:

```python
@app.route('/my_protected/<verify:int>')
@protected()
async def my_protected(request: Request, verify):
    print(bool(verify))
```
with request path `/my_protected/0/`

Maybe this doesn't look like a serious vulnerability but of course it is not a good thing and should be fixed.
`*args **kwargs` from request mustn't clash with internal params, because of unpredictable result and possible security issues. Currently these params are not used, but if there is a need to keep them for future and extensibility we could avoid bad things by using extra request_args and request_kwargs params and pass them as usual keyword arguments.

I have tested this approach a bit, see https://github.com/ahopkins/sanic-jwt/compare/master...CaptFrozenberg:master. Maybe there are more places where we could use such kind of solution. If this looks ok, I can do a PR, and write a few notes about websockets in docs.

Yes, this is what could be happening: there is an average of ~5000 unsent messages accumulated per thread, and they all get lost because the shutdown procedure does not trigger `logging.shutdown()` for whatever reason. `logging.shutdown()` is supposed to fire automatically on interpreter exit: https://hg.python.org/cpython/file/2.7/Lib/logging/__init__.py#l1685.

If you have an application exit handler routine, could you try inserting `logging.shutdown()` there? Hopefully that will fix things. Perhaps the atexit handlers don't get fired properly in daemon threads. If that's the case, I'll have to find a general solution. I need to understand better how atexit handlers interact with threading.

Hi Joe, as I recall, this feature is somewhere in partial implementation status, probably most suitable for writing a hyperlink rather than reading.

A Run object has a read-only `hyperlink` property that provides a _Hyperlink object. _Hyperlink.address will be None if there is no actual hyperlink (hlinkClick element) present. So you should be able to detect hyperlinks by testing the address property. If you get something it will be the address, at least in the case of a URL. Not sure exactly what you get if it's an internal link to a different slide.

So something like this air code I imagine:

```python
shape = ... shape you want to look in
for paragraph in shape.text_frame.paragraphs:
    for run in paragraph.runs:
        hyperlink = run.hyperlink
        if hyperlink.address is not None:
            print(hyperlink.address)
```

Let us know how you go and where it falls short if it does, you can probably get further by going down to the lxml layer from hyperlink to get more. Once you have some partially working code we can help point you in the right direction.

The space-filling algorithm that is used basically tries to place words one after another. This will preserve ranks of words, but not necessarily respect frequencies. If there is not enough space to place a word the font will be made smaller. You can guarantee that all words will get the same size if you set the maximum font size small enough so that all words can be drawn without decreasing font-size.

Your `dataset` library I didn't know, looks interesting. It's not a replacement for dict though -- the syntax is different. If you're OK with committing to a special API, why not just go with MongoDB = excellent Python support.

`sqlitedict` is for those cases where you're using dict, find out it consumes too much RAM, so switch to a DB backend without having to rewrite everything. It doesn't need any external DB (sqlite3 is an in-process DB, with built-in support in Python). It adds a few extra features on top, such as allowing multithreaded access (the sqlite3 adaptor in Python would normally just fail), but that's basically it. > Is it possible for CherryPy to drop this dependency and internalize it?

It's possible, but doing so would effectively blacklist all namespace packages from use in any popular package or any package that wishes to be popular.

I would very much like not to have to blacklist namespace packages. It's true that the way namespace packages are defined and installed is to omit the `__init__.py` file and instead provide native support (Python 3.5+) or have a compatibility hook (the `-nspkg.pth` file) for older Pythons.

But since pip installs those things properly, the packages themselves should work fine:

```
$ python -m venv issues-2
Collecting jaraco.classes
  Using cached jaraco.classes-1.4.3-py2.py3-none-any.whl
Collecting six (from jaraco.classes)
  Using cached six-1.11.0-py2.py3-none-any.whl
Installing collected packages: six, jaraco.classes
Successfully installed jaraco.classes-1.4.3 six-1.11.0
$ issues-2/bin/python -c "import jaraco.classes.properties"
$ cat issues-2/lib/python3.6/site-packages/jaraco.classes-1.4.3-py3.6-nspkg.pth 
import sys, types, os;has_mfs = sys.version_info > (3, 5);p = os.path.join(sys._getframe(1).f_locals['sitedir'], *('jaraco',));importlib = has_mfs and __import__('importlib.util');has_mfs and __import__('importlib.machinery');m = has_mfs and sys.modules.setdefault('jaraco', importlib.util.module_from_spec(importlib.machinery.PathFinder.find_spec('jaraco', [os.path.dirname(p)])));m = m or sys.modules.setdefault('jaraco', types.ModuleType('jaraco'));mp = (m or []) and m.__dict__.setdefault('__path__',[]);(p not in mp) and mp.append(p)
$ ls issues-2/lib/python3.6/site-packages/jaraco                            
classes
```

AFAIK, this package (and other jaraco.* and keyrings.* packages) is doing all the right things to support namespace packages.

Years ago, I would have been more hesitant to introduce namespace packages to a project's dependencies because of the cases where they weren't supported. If we continue to pull those packages from the dependency chain, those projects (celery, pex) won't have any incentive to fix the bug. Indeed, it sort-of feels like the request here is for me to relegate all namespace packages to second class status in perpetuity.

Given that namespace packages have been a concept for a long time and that the [jaraco namespace has been around for most of a decade](https://github.com/jaraco/jaraco.util/tree/52348a76b4e67711f9c9abe29d77751e50270f4b), and given that namespace packages are a standard baked into Python, I think it really makes sense for these alternate packaging systems to fix thee issues they have with supporting these packages.

If you have constructive comments, I'm happy to help, but as filed, this ticket is essentially a request to withdraw this project, which is a non-starter.

I finally found the problem: it had to do not with the referencing itself, but with the breaking lines relation among the images. I will explain since this may help others:
In the examples I provided, I removed  a line because I thought it would only distract. This is how my text was originally:
MD text:
```
built that extend the possibilities of the initial set ([@fig:b03; @fig:b04]).

![graph of the iterative process](./res/monos-b-01.png){#fig:b01 width=30%}
![Example of emerging components](./res/monos-b-03.png){#fig:b03 width=30%}

![Additional example of emerging components](./res/monos-b-04.png){#fig:b04 }
```
I removed the line containing the fig b01, because I thought it was not related to the problem and would make reading easier. I ran into this caveat:

> This only works on implicit figures, i.e. an image occurring by itself in a paragraph (which will be rendered as a figure with caption by pandoc)
(from https://hackage.haskell.org/package/pandoc-crossref-0.2.5.0)

My figure is not strictly within the text, but it had a single break instead of a double break, which the working figure does have. Probably the MD parser considered the single break as no break at all. This is sad, because the nice thing about writing in MD is not having to deal with counter-intuitive problems that other formats may have. 

In short, the way to solve the not-found figure references is to separate all the target figures with at least to line breaks.

I checked the python `zipfile` docs, and turning on `allowZip64=True` looks safe:

* it only affects the sending side (ZipFile writer): the ZipFile reader always accepts Zip64-mode files, so we don't need to add anything to the Wormhole protocol to prepare the receiver for a mode switch
* it's implemented entirely in Python: so all platforms can use it
* according to the debian `zip` docs, Zip64 mode is needed when any individual file is larger than 4GB, the overall archive is larger than 4GB, or there are more than 64K files in the archive. It has slightly more overhead (larger headers, etc), so it's only used if necessary.

The only reason `zipfile` doesn't turn it on by default is because the unix `/usr/bin/zip` and `unzip` utilities didn't originally support Zip64 mode. But from what I can find in the various Changelog files, that was at least 10 years ago, and magic-wormhole doesn't use an external `unzip` program anyways, so we don't need to be compatible with one. Also, the `zipfile` module switched the `Zip64=` default to True starting in python3.4, so they must have come to the same conclusion.

Let's continue the discussion here. I don't get in any way how tilde rendering is relevant to nbsp or combining characters... The purpose of the codec is to have a simple & logical one-to-one correspondence between unicode characters and latex commands. There are simple tilde characters in both unicode and latex so we simply need to find a logical way of connecting these latex commands:

```
\textasciitilde
\~{}
\texttildelow
$\sim$
```

with these unicode symbols:

```
~
\N{SMALL TILDE}
\N{MODIFIED LETTER LOW TILDE}
\N{TILDE OPERATOR}
```

A bit more googling for "latex text centered tilde" lead me to http://tex.stackexchange.com/a/2883 so now I'm pretty convinced that it makes sense to connect ``\textasciitilde`` to a plain unicode ~.

So I suggest to connect them in the order as given above. This is simple and understandable, no surprises anywhere. The fact that ``\texttildelow`` gives a non-low tilde in some fonts is arguably a bug as well (just look at the name of the command...).

Honestly, I still prefer the `Consumer` class.

```python
queue = yield from connect_and_declare_queue()
qc = queue.consume(no_ack=True)
yield from qc.get()
```
```python
async def start():
    queue = await connect_and_declare_queue()
    async for msg in queue.consume(no_ack=True):
        # Process message
```

With both of these proposals, the interface of `consume` depends on the arguments you passed in to it. In the first instance, if you pass no callback then it returns you something from which you can `get` messages; if you do pass a callback then you just get something you can cancel (but calling `get` on the result doesn't make sense). The second proposal is even stranger - if you don't pass in a callback then you get an async iterable back; if you do you get. (How do you `cancel` such a consumer?)

With the `QueuingConsumer`, `consume` _always_ takes a callback and _always_ returns something you can use to `cancel` it; you can _optionally_ turn on extra features such as error handling by adding methods to the callback object.

----------

```python
def consume(callback, *, error_callback=None, no_local=False, no_ack=False, exclusive=False, arguments=None)
```
This is not a bad idea; it's roughly isomorphic to the `Consumer` object I'm proposing. (Your proposal is to pass multiple functions; in the other option you effectively pass a record of functions.) How do we decide between the two? The `Consumer` object works best when the `on_error` callback naturally belongs as part of the same object as the message callback. The `error_callback` proposal works best when the two functions are unrelated. Which of these two use-cases is the more common?

Note that you can fairly straightforwardly convert between the two interfaces:

```python
# one way
class ConsumerAdapter:  # or something
    def __init__(self, callback, error_callback):
        self._callback = callback
        self._error_callback = error_callback
    def __call__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        return self._callback(*args, **kwargs)
    def on_error(self, *args, **kwargs):
        return self._error_callback(*args, **kwargs)

queue.consume(ConsumerAdapter(my_callback, my_error_callback))
```
```python
# the other way
queue.consume(my_consumer, error_callback=my_consumer.on_error)
```

So the question is, _which do we want to be more awkward_ - the case when your two callbacks are unrelated (you have to wrap them in an object) or the case when your two callbacks naturally form an object (you have to unpack the methods and pass them in)?

----------

When you register your model with reversion, you are providing a "follow"
argument.

For each model that has a "follow" argument, does that "follow" list a
property on that model which might contain a related model with a primary
key of None?

For example:

class ReallyBadIdea:

    @property
    def so_bad(self):
        return User()

reversion.register(ReallyBadIdea, follow=["so_bad"])

In this case, when reversion, follows the ReallyBadIdeam class, it will end
up tracking a User instance with no primary key.

On the other hand, specifying the name of a built-in models.ForeignKey()
field will never have a model with a primary key of None.

@spad if you are only sending the cropped area of the face, you need to tell it that the whole image contains the face. If you don't, it will search for the face within the image and won't know the whole image itself is the face. If it can't find a face inside the image, it won't be able to do anything.

So just tell it that the whole image is the face:

```python
import face_recognition

img = face_recognition.load_image_file("your_cropped_image")

# Assume the whole image is the location of the face
height, width, _ = img.shape
# location is in css order - top, right, bottom, left
face_location = (0, width, height, 0)

encodings = face_recognition.face_encodings(img, known_face_locations=[face_location])
```

Note: I didn't really test this code, so just double check that the results it gives you are correct.

It actually is going to get more complicated as email addresses become more internationalized. The rules for internationalization are different for the local part and domain part of addresses. In the local part, complex Unicode input from end users should be normalized to a canonical Unicode form. For example, if a user enters two code points to achieve an accented letter, that's the same email address as when the accented letter is represented by a single pre-composed code point.

I worked through this while trying to build an email address validation module:

https://github.com/JoshData/python-email-validator

I'd be interested to find a way to get Django to do this properly, to ensure that a) no user accounts are created with invalid email addresses, and b) the right normalizations are in place at user account creation (to prevent non-uniqueness) and at login (in case the user enters a different character string but equivalent address). I took a stab at this in https://github.com/if-then-fund/django-betteruser (same purpose as this repository) but I'd rather work with others on solving this rather than inventing my own user model. :)

Hi Chris,

They are beautiful indeed! I'd say a better way is to do this is to use the plot_trisurf, which uses the Mesh widget.
The problem with lines in Open/Web GL is that you either draw a strip (where you connect all the subsequent vertices, or rather indices to the vertices) or a series of lines (from vertex index i, to j, then from k to l, ...) and cannot say draw from index i to j and then to k, and then one from l to m.
So to get the best performance (I think) is to draw this using a series of lines, lets take an example code.
```
ipv.figure()
x = np.arange(5) 
y = x**0.5
z = x**2
# draws two lines segments, one from 0->1->2 and one from 3->4
ipv.plot_trisurf(x, y, z, lines=np.array([0,1,1,2,3,4]))
ipv.show()
```
![lines](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1765949/30153294-6b0e1764-93b6-11e7-9b2e-9dc9acb34bb5.png)

Here the lines reference the vertex index, so no vertex (x,y,z) position will be duplicated.

> I'm not sure why indexing='ij' isn't the default behavior for meshgrid, but it makes the most sense to me.
The dimensions are actually ordered logically. X[:,j] == x and Y[i,:] == y for any i, j.

I think this goes back to C being [row-major order, aka C-order](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Row-_and_column-major_order) as well as numpy having that as default. This means that the last index advances to the next memory address, and which also corresponds in framebuffers to the next column (x coordinate). Also, that's what you see with matplotlib's imshow as well.

For volshow, it gets confusing because of meshgrid, it's docstring says:
> In the 3-D case with inputs of length M, N and P, outputs are of shape (N, M, P) for
'xy' indexing and (M, N, P) for 'ij' indexing. 

Again, volshow, like imshow, expects C-order, so it wants in this case shape (P, N, M), but the xy indexing (the default) is quite odd. But using ij indexing and a transpose should get us where we want.
```python
x = linspace(0,1,11)
y = linspace(0,1,11)
z = linspace(0,1,11)
X, Y, Z = meshgrid(x,y,z, indexing='ij')
ipv.figure()
ipv.volshow(X.T) # or Y.T or Z.T
ipv.show()
```
This is what you found out as well!

I think maybe the confusion with matplotlib is that the z direction is up, in ipyvolume is points out of the screen. This is the default OpenGL/WebGL convention, x and y are like in 2d, we keep a right handed coordinate system, so z should point out of the screen. I actually prefer having z pointing up (by default), and making this configurable.
Hope this answers your questions and/or confusion, it also confuses me from time to time!

```
    def __call__(self, environ, start_response):
        start = clock()
        status_codes = []

        def custom_start_response(status, response_headers, exc_info=None):
            status_codes.append(int(status.partition(' ')[0]))
            for name, value in response_headers:
                if name.lower() == 'content-length':
                    content_lengths.append(int(value))
                    break
            return start_response(status, response_headers, exc_info)
        retval = self.application(environ, custom_start_response)
        try:
            counter = 0
            for i in retval:
                yield i
                counter += len(i)
        finally:
            runtime = int((clock() - start) * 10**6)
            content_length = content_lengths[0] if content_lengths else counter
            msg = self.formatter(status_codes[0], environ, content_length, ip_header=self.ip_header, rt_us=runtime)
            self.logger.info(msg)
```

I've definitely faced that importing issue. It's a real pain.

What's happening is this: When you enqueue a task, it might get run by an instance that's already been used for other stuff (eg: the instance that enqueued it), or a new instance that's just been spun up, and it's the first thing to run.

If it runs on an older instance, it's probably going to be fine; all the modules you'll use are loaded, your whole initialisation process has run.

But on a brand new instance, you might be entirely skipping the normal initialisation process for your code. So you can get errors like this:

PermanentTaskFailure: No module named app.Report

because a particular module was never imported, or you can get "cannot import" errors that might indicate that your vendoring code is being skipped, etc.


If you are importing @task in the way I describe in the @task article, ie: via a handler like this:

```
handlers:
    - url: /_ah/task/.*
      script: taskutils.app
      login: admin

``` 
then this is probably happening to you. You can see it's saying to go load the module taskutils.py (likely from a lib file via vendoring) and pass the call to the global "app" object in there. 

If you've got a big, complex app, with something like a main.py that you use for most calls, all your imports probably happen there. That's certainly the case for me. But if you use the handler as above, that main.py never gets loaded if the instance is new, imports don't happen, stuff breaks.

There are some alternative ways to wire @task into your app. I think you're using flask, so checkout the bottom of task.py:

```
def setuptasksforflask(flaskapp):
    @flaskapp.route(_DEFAULT_FLASK_URL, methods=["POST"])
    def taskhandler(fmodule, ffunction):
        _launch_task(flask.request.data, "%s/%s" % (fmodule, ffunction), flask.request.headers)
        return ""

```
If you've got some central _main.py_ or similar that normally handles your calls, and constructs your flask app, you can just add this line:

```
  from taskutils import setuptasksforflask

  # do stuff that creates your flask app, "app".

  setuptasksforflask(app)

```
then change your app.yaml handler to be

```
handlers:
    - url: /_ah/task/.*
      script: main.app
      login: admin
```

Now @task call are correctly constructing your whole app if that hasn't happened already, and you should see these import issues go away.

Lazy initialization for each row in the row_sparse weight parameter is an optimization we haven't done yet. You're right - ideally the weight can be initialized only when a certain category is seen. Currently all rows are filled at one shot during initialization. What sparse embedding provides is the ability to only retrieve the parameters for the categories in the current mini-batch (instead of loading the full model). 
For example, you might have a full model on CPU. For each mini-batch, only load the rows for the seen categories on GPU and perform forward backward. 

Lazy initialization is definitely worth investigations though. What's your use case / application?

We don't really want to make it stateful.  I'm assuming you want to avoid remembering the flags, so here are some options:

- If you already have a Makefile, just add a `watch` rule.  We do this in watchman itself: https://github.com/facebook/watchman/blob/master/Makefile.am#L215
- You can write a little shell script that just runs watchman-make with the right flags for your project.  You could call it `watch.sh` and commit it into your repo alongside the rest of your code in that project
- You can use the alias feature of your shell to make a shortcut so that you don't need to think or type so hard next time around

Your build will use the underlying kevent/kqueue system facility for file change notifications.  We used to use this by default in earlier versions of Watchman.  It is also used on FreeBSD and OpenBSD.   I ran our integration test on my 10.10 installation but with kevent enabled and it passed its tests, so you should have no functional problems.

You may however need to raise your system limits to accommodate watching the directories of interest.
The default limit on OS X is very low.  Watchman will try to raise it on startup; you'll see something like this in your log file:

```
2015-08-15T15:21:06,279: [listener] file limit is 256 kern.maxfilesperproc=10240
```

This means that you can watch up to `10240` (or whatever number you see on that line) total files and directories before you hit the system limit.  If Watchman is unable to raise the file limit to the system limit, you'll see another line with an error message.

If you hit the system limit, watchman will be unable to notify you of changes, which means that ember won't automatically rebuild things as you make changes.

You can raise the limit by following the instruction that are in red in this changeset:

https://github.com/facebook/watchman/commit/20dbfd48ffc5b41c506b12b79ce9c4d00e54d31d#diff-e94ba8063cbe3fc47d19c80ef488d161

This isn't a problem with boto3 you're just trying to download boto3 with pip and pip is failing to do so.

That being said I do happen to know a bit about that error so:

You are using the system python which is linked to an old version of OpenSSL that won't be able to contact PyPI. You updated OpenSSL through homebrew but that doesn't do anything to the python install that was already compiled linked to the old version. You can verify that this is the case by doing:
```
$ python
Python 2.7.10 (default, Oct 23 2015, 19:19:21)
[GCC 4.2.1 Compatible Apple LLVM 7.0.0 (clang-700.0.59.5)] on darwin
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> import ssl
>>> print(ssl.OPENSSL_VERSION)
OpenSSL 0.9.8zh 14 Jan 2016    <--- this is the outdated version that ships with OS X.
```

You should not be using the system python since that will always be linked to the old OpenSSL, instead you should install python through some other means and when it is compiled link to the new homebrew supplied OpenSSL. 

You have a few options. You can install python through brew itself and specify that you want to link against the homebrew installed version of OpenSSL: `brew install python --with-brewed-openssl`. 

Or if you prefer to manage your python installations yourself (as I do) you can make sure that the following environment variables are set when compiling python:
```
CPPFLAGS="-I$(brew --prefix openssl)/include"
LDFLAGS="-L$(brew --prefix openssl)/lib"
```

Once either of those options are done, the pip that is associated with that python installation will work correctly.

I had some time to look into this.  Broadly, this behavior is the goal:

- Normalize package names with pep423 etc when _we_ write them to the pipfile (e.g. someone calls `pipenv install` from the command line)
- Normalize all package names written to the lockfile
- Return the normalized name for comparison against other packages in the index
- *Don't* normalize anything we didn't write -- if someone manually writes a lockfile, leave the names alone including capitalization etc

> What's wrong with putting them relative to sys.prefix as distutils does?

The main problem with allowing `easy_install` to install files other than to a single location is that violates that explicit expectation. `easy_install` was designed to put an egg in a single location such that deleting the egg would readily delete the package (much in the same way that deleting a directory in /Applications on a Mac will uninstall that application). There has been the one long-standing exception that scripts would additionally be installed to `bin`, but even those were wrappers linking to the functionality in the package, such that no meaningful content remains after removing an egg.

I'm happy to allow a package to install arbitrary files to arbitrary directories (perhaps under `$prefix`) but it should be clear that one cannot expect `easy_install` to facilitate the uninstall and that one _must_ use pip (or another tool) to keep track of installed files.

I would say that shrinking the list would be the best idea. It would be best to drop the regional locales in all cases where the difference is only capitalization, and ideally if technically possible, also drop them in the cases where there are only small differences in spelling. Most of the cases, you will want to be able to parse the small variations of spelling that occur between regions, even if you are parsing say a website in Brazil or Portugal. In English, you would want to "color" be considered the same as "colour" even if you are parsing a British website.

```python
class MySpider(Spider):
    def __init__(self, domains=None):
```

----------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------
NEW                   | commit adds a new feature
FIX 	                   | commit fixes a bug
BETTER  	           | commit improves an existing feature
COMPATIBILITY | compatibility remarks, removing features and dependencies
NOTE 	           | any general note (if needed)

A low number of labels could have direct translation to coloured labelled badges in the release notes, e.g. green for new, blue for better, orange for fixes, red for compatibility notes, gray for general notes.

(And if we want to have emojis, there are many ways how to find an association between  these labels and the [available set](http://www.emoji-cheat-sheet.com/); though personally I think the short uppercase textual labels might be sufficient.)

Just wanted to let you know that as this is material which I was pretty intimately involved in writing and testing (the latter involving tackling a few tricky, conceptual fourier space issues with the help of others on the team) I'm happy to help as much as I can in explaining anything in the old code that needs it - I'll try keep a closer eye on my gmail to help with this.

@rmandelb I think you're right - the sinc interpolation will be slow.  Actually, at the heart of how correlated noise stuff is used in galsim is an approximate rendering of the noise correlation function using the blinear interpolant (see, e.g., lines around ~990 in `correlated_noise.py`... there's also a Github discussion of this very point somewhere, I'll try to find it...).  This is a fudge, one that works well enough in practice so far, as evidenced by our tests, but that would fail if you wanted to make an image of HST correlated noise at *super* HST resolution, for example.

(The problem is that an image noise correlation function as a mathematical surface brightness profile is really a load of delta functions convolved with galsim.Pixel objects... The power spectrum is infinitely repeating.  The DFT handles this all naturally, once you've got a reasonably accurate image of the correlation function to start from.  It may be that `drawKImage` does too, but I'm not really knowledgeable about what it does!)

> abc is a registry based metaclass system allowing things to claim subclass status without sharing any code

Yep. I want a system where things can claim to be the same type (fsvo 'type') without sharing code. Another way to approximate this is fully abstract classes (like the one you specified), but unlike `_BinaryComparison` (which some people would call an abstract type).

We can do that, although need to be clear in the documentation that we mean "something that implements this protocol" rather than "something for which `isinstance(x, Foo)` holds". 

> I am confused though - Mismatch is immutable today (to the extent that anything in Python is) - none of its code mutates state, nor that of anything accessed via it. And nothing consuming it assumes mutability either.

It's not documented as immutable, and as such any subclass can rely on changing state, perhaps by manipulating `_description` and `_details` (elsewhere in testtools, an underscore prefix means "public to subclasses").

If we changed it to use something like `pyrsistent.PClass`, e.g.

```python
class Mismatch(PClass):
  _description = field(text)
  _details = field(pmap)

  # ... methods here
```

Then almost all of the matchers in testtools would work fine, but some wouldn't. It'd be an API break, because we allow subclassing.

So… I think we're converging on fully-abstract-but-otherwise-vanilla classes?

It isn't possible, in Cloudformation, to set the name of an Autoscaling group.  In general, when dealing with Cloudformation (and by extension troposphere) it's generally better to not name things yourselves, as you can run into all sorts of issues with updates.

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSCloudFormation/latest/UserGuide/aws-properties-name.html

Note: The name of the autoscaling group is prefixed by a combination of `<stack_name>-<resource_name>`.  So if you name the stack `my-prod-stack` and the AutoscalingGroup resource `ASG1`, the prefix of the autoscaling group created would be `my-prod-stack-ASG1`, followed by some randomly generated characters for uniqueness.

@christopheblin 
I've just wrapped up a project where we implemented CI/CD using SAM tooling. I recommend part of the build process (whether manual or automated) include a step to publish your build artifacts into an artifact repository. Each time you package, you can package up the lambda code into a different versioned S3 bucket, and then push the package.yml file itself up into the S3 bucket. That way, those S3 files can be reused to do `sam deploy` for any versioned artifact that exist in your S3 bucket.

For example, here's a snippet from a Jenkinsfile groovy script:
```
                // We deploy to a different folder scheme depending on master/feature. Let's logic.
                base_folder     = "release-build-artifacts/${serviceName}/${releaseVersion}"
                lambda_folder  = "${base_folder}/lambda"
                cfn_folder      = "${base_folder}/cfn"
                
                // SAM Package
                sh "sam package --template-file ${service_name}.sam.yml \
                --s3-bucket ${s3_bucket} \
                --s3-prefix ${lambda_folder} \
                --output-template-file build/${service_name}.package.yml"
                sh "aws s3 cp build/${service_name}.package.yml s3://${s3_bucket}/${cfn_folder}"
```
You'll have to fill in the parameters for "${serviceName}", "${releaseVersion}", and "${s3_bucket}"

> Would like to suggest that we track a detailed and thorough release note chapter for each release

Notes from discussion at today's dev meeting:

* There are user needs for both a high-level executive summary of changes that can be skimmed, as well as a detailed technical overview with links to the code-base that can be searched and analyzed.
* We're already diligent about adding github milestones to every issue and PR, which creates a detailed and complete overview of everything that happened in a release. However, this overview is too noisy for general consumption.
* Asking developers to manually update another (more detailed) changelog would introduce too much friction to the development process, especially given that most information is already contained in the github milestone overview. 

Next steps:

I'll add a new github label named <kbd>changelog</kbd>. I'll also update our PR template to include an action item like

 * [ ] If this change results in a salient user-facing change, the PR or a related issue is tagged with the **_changelog_** label.

Before future releases, we'll go through and make sure that all items with the 'changelog' label have clear, meaningful titles. Then, from the high-level changelog, we can link to a github overview which filters by milestone + changelog label.

> I'd rather have several short, one-purpose decorators than a long, single, multi-purpose one.

I would tend to agree with this, except that these behaviors *already* seem very closely coupled throughout `client.py`. It seems (at least so far), everywhere that `@check_resource` is used, the code in the OP is repeated (or if it isn't, it was intended to be). Just look at how many places I removed those lines from `client.py`.

> if we put it here the condition will also be unnecessarily evaluated in the case of image-oriented functions. Seems like a bad practice even though the overhead is negligible.

Perhaps, but this is already the case. Well before I came along, `@check_resource` already pulled double duty between containers and images. Perhaps it makes more sense to have nearly identical, but separately named decorators for images and containers (e.g., `@check_image` and `@check_container`). I can do that, but that seems to be a more significant change than this was intended to be.

Plus, the overhead may be comparable when stacking the same two decorators everywhere:

```py
@check_container
@check_id
def attach(self, container, ...):
    ...
```

I know that wasn't your original point though.

All I really meant to do here was an incremental improvement towards DRY without disturbing the status quo too much. Ultimately, it's your call whether such a thing is worth merging. Either way, the fact that the code in the OP shows up in so many places is probably indicative that *something* needs to be done.

I don't exactly have a clear plan, but I learned a lot about the different options available in Beaker.  Most of them don't work because AllenNLP cannot interpret environment variables as non-strings and because Beaker functionality is missing some necessary components.

On the AllenNLP side, I think we should close out the easy parts of https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/issues/447 and add overrides.  On the Beaker side, they prefer coming up with an easier way to specify experiments on the command line.

Here are some notes about different potential approaches.

```
Methods of doing a parameter search with AllenNLP and Beaker:

* Manually run a series of experiments and aggregate the results.

This is what Tushar does presently.  This has full flexibility but requires
manually aggregating the results.

* Create a batched experiment and manually create permutations of the configuration.

Pro's: nice Beaker visualization and fine-grained control.
Con's: labor intensive to create each dataset, does not scale to hundreds of permutations.

http://beaker.allenai.org/ex/ex_8dm8xov2wj7v

description: "Example wordcount experiment"
tasks:
  - name: snli2
    spec:
      description: Train SNLI with 2 epochs
      image: allennlp/allennlp:v0.3.0
      resultPath: '/output'
      args:
        - 'allennlp/run'
        - 'train'
        - '/config.json'
        - '-s'
        - '/output'
      datasetMounts:
        - datasetId: snli_1_train_short.1
          containerPath: /training.jsonl
        - datasetId: ds_w0e4m4zphjxm
          containerPath: /config.json

  - name: snli3
    spec:
      description: Train SNLI with 3 epochs
      image: allennlp/allennlp:v0.3.0
      resultPath: /output
      args:
        - 'allennlp/run'
        - 'train'
        - '/config.json'
        - '-s'
        - '/output'
      datasetMounts:
        - datasetId: snli_1_train_short.1
          containerPath: /training.jsonl
        - datasetId: ds_qy9voivhii0v
          containerPath: /config.json

  - name: snli4
    spec:
      description: Train SNLI with 4 epochs
      image: allennlp/allennlp:v0.3.0
      resultPath: /output
      args:
        - 'allennlp/run'
        - 'train'
        - '/config.json'
        - '-s'
        - '/output'
      datasetMounts:
        - datasetId: snli_1_train_short.1
          containerPath: /training.jsonl
        - datasetId: ds_3cnl9qmexud8
          containerPath: /config.json

* Create a batched experiment and create permutations with overrides.

pro: nice Beaker visualization and fine-grained control.
pro: a single dataset can be used for every configuration.
con: you still need to create a big yaml file that largely just repeats itself.
con: overrides isn't impelmented on train (but easily could be, see https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/pull/402)

description: "Example wordcount experiment"
tasks:
  - name: snli2
    spec:
      description: Train SNLI with 2 epochs
      image: allennlp/allennlp:v0.3.0
      resultPath: '/output'
      args:
        - 'allennlp/run'
        - 'train'
        - '/config.json'
        - '-s'
        - '/output'
        - --overrides '{ "trainer.num_epochs": 4 }'
      datasetMounts:
        - datasetId: snli_1_train_short.1
          containerPath: /training.jsonl
        - datasetId: XYZ
          containerPath: /config.json

  - name: snli3
    spec:
      description: Train SNLI with 3 epochs
      image: allennlp/allennlp:v0.3.0
      resultPath: /output
      args:
        - 'allennlp/run'
        - 'train'
        - '/config.json'
        - '-s'
        - '/output'
        - --overrides '{ "trainer.num_epochs": 4 }'
      datasetMounts:
        - datasetId: snli_1_train_short.1
          containerPath: /training.jsonl
        - datasetId: XYZ
          containerPath: /config.json

  - name: snli4
    spec:
      description: Train SNLI with 4 epochs
      image: allennlp/allennlp:v0.3.0
      resultPath: /output
      args:
        - 'allennlp/run'
        - 'train'
        - '/config.json'
        - '-s'
        - '/output'
        - --overrides '{ "trainer.num_epochs": 4 }'
      datasetMounts:
        - datasetId: snli_1_train_short.1
          containerPath: /training.jsonl
        - datasetId: XYZ
          containerPath: /config.json

* Grid search

Con's: cannot specify the arguments I need so I would need to create a custom Docker image.
Con's: limiting and custom configuration language for setting up searches.
Con's: AllenNLP does not support training overrides.  Overrides are needed because otherwise environment variables are interpreted as strings.
Pro's: compact representation.

{
  "envs": [
    {
      "name": "EPOCHS",
      "range": {"start": 2, "step": 1, "end": 4}
    }
  ]
}

(inside Docker image set run command to the following)

python -m allennlp.run train /config.json -s /output --overrides "{ \"trainer.num_epochs\": $EPOCHS }'

* Manually create an experiment group

Con's: AllenNLP does not support training overrides.  Overrides are needed because otherwise environment variables are interpreted as strings.

beaker group create snli_epoch
for EPOCH in {2..4}
do
    EX=$(beaker experiment run --result-path /output --gpu-count=1 --source XYZ:/config.json --detach allennlp/allennlp:v0.3.0 allennlp/run train /config.json -s /output --overrides '{ "trainer.num_epochs": $EPOCH}')
    beaker group add snli_epoch $EX
done
```

Methods like `pop_int` would be used in all of our `from_params` methods where the code is explicitly expecting an `int`.  They actually improve our type safety and code readability, they are just annoying to implement, because we have a lot of code already written.  It's not an issue of the user wanting a string with an integer value.

Another snag in this approach is if you want to have the learning rate be specified by an environment variable (which, if you're doing a grid search with beaker, is _definitely_ something you would want to do).  Those parameters get passed through to pytorch without a `pop` at all - we'd have to write wrappers for all of the optimizers and whatever else, which is even more annoying.

A second one could be accomplished by using a 'group-by' sort of processor, which takes a sorted stream and splits it to multiple streams based on a "key" (composed out of values in specific columns).

Annoying thing about Travis: even when in the py3 parts of the build matrix, `virtualenv` still sets up a py2 virtualenv. That's why the travisfile wasn't using `make test/opts`.

You can confirm which python is being used by looking at which site-packages folder is used (inside of `.../python2.7/...`). I would _love it_ if you can figure out a way to have the Makefile be Travis-friendly, but that blew up in my face when I last tried.

We haven't paid much attention to these commands as it's not clear whether or not we should keep them.
There's a case to be made that even the `output_format` option isn't necessary, and no other config parameters have been added or surfaced as desirable.

I'll put this and the other `globus config` issues into the `1.0` milestone, but we should take a moment to discuss the future of these commands.

> It was not clear from the "config init" help that it would interactively prompt me to set user-definable configuration values, namely for output_format. But is the "config init" even needed? It seems redundant with "config set", aside from its interactive nature. But I suppose if there are more user-definable options, that would be useful.

My desire for that command was based on my own experience with `knife`. The `knife init` command is more or less essential, as there are six or seven options which you must set to even get started with the tool, and without the interactive prompts I can't imagine doing it correctly every time.

The same rules may not apply to us; I'm not sure.

Looks good. As a separate issue, should we also log the level at which transfers are running? I think it would be a good `INFO` log message to have, so that if you are suspicious that your sync level is being passed through correctly, you can check.

We only send header requests to the peer we've chosen (the one with highest TD) to sync with. Once we have a batch of headers we'll request body parts for subsets of them to all available peers

I am setting up a virtual environment on Windows 10 with the latest Anaconda (conda version : 4.3.21) and python 2.7 using an environment file and also ran into this problem. I did activate the root environment prior to creating the new environment. 

Looking at the environment variables in the root environment I noticed that PYTHONIOENCODING was missing. After manually setting this variable equal to the codepage currently used by the cmd shell (in this case it was 1252) I was able to successfully create the new environment. When I started up the new environment it threw the same codepage error (LookupError: unknown encoding: cp65001) until I manually set the PYTHONIOENCODING environment variable.

I took a look at the activate.bat script and noticed the PYTHONIOENCODING variable was not persisted to the interactive cmd shell environment. I added the following line at line 83 in activate.bat:
    @SET "PYTHONIOENCODING=%PYTHONIOENCODING%"

I closed down my virtual environments and restarted them and everything started working as expected. I then removed my new virtual environment and tried re-creating it from the root environment and it worked as expected. The newly created virtual environment worked without error.

Was persisting the PYTHONIOENCODING environment variable to the interactive shell inadvertently left out of the activate.bat script? Or is there a reason for not including it? Without this line in activate.bat I am unable to properly create or use python 2.7  virtual environments on Windows 10. I am writing instructions for non-technical users to perform this setup and I would prefer not to require them to edit the activate.bat script. At this point using python 3 for the virtual environment is not an option because one of the libraries that will be installed has not been updated to work correctly with python 3.

Any help/insight you could provide would be greatly appreciated.

Actually, here is a fix that makes all of @amueller's examples work:

```
def knn_initialize(X, missing_mask, verbose=False, min_dist=1e-6):
    """
    Fill X with NaN values if necessary, construct the n_samples x n_samples
    distance matrix and set the self-distance of each row to infinity.
    """
    X_row_major = X.copy("C")
    if missing_mask.sum() != np.isnan(X_row_major).sum():
        # if the missing values have already been zero-filled need
        # to put NaN's back in the data matrix for the distances function
        X_row_major[missing_mask] = np.nan
    D = all_pairs_normalized_distances(X_row_major, verbose=verbose)
    # set diagonal of distance matrix to infinity since we don't want
    # points considering themselves as neighbors
    max_dist = 1e6 * np.maximum(1, D.max())
    for i in range(X.shape[0]):
        D[i, i] = max_dist
    D[D < min_dist] = min_dist
    D[D > max_dist] = max_dist
    return X_row_major, D
```

Starting in version 1.4 of Elasticsearch, there's an internal test—which is accessed by this API—that verifies that _all_ nodes of the cluster have write access to the snapshot repository.  Curator uses this to prevent you from attempting a snapshot which would have automatically been `PARTIAL` (because not all nodes could write to the shared repository).

What kind of repository are you using?  `fs`? `s3`?

Ah, you're using SVG figures. I'd guess that when the figures disappear, it's because the notebook has become untrusted. Are you running it on different computers, synchronised by something like Dropbox or Github?

Output formats like HTML and SVG are able to send code to the kernel to execute, so we have to be careful about displaying them, because it's a security risk. If a notebook is recognised as one that you have run all of and saved on this computer before, it is *trusted*, and those outputs are displayed. If any of the outputs weren't generated and saved on this computer, it's untrusted, and you can't see potentially dangerous outputs.

Possible workarounds:
- Use PNG plots - PNGs can't send code to the kernel (except potentially by exploiting browser bugs), so they don't need to be trusted.
- If you're sure of where the notebook came from, you can manually mark it as trusted in the file menu.

Here is what I found to work for me:

If you just want the latest [published version from PyPI](https://pypi.org/project/kubernetes-py/), run `pip3 install kubernetes-py` to have the project and its dependencies downloaded and installed. Specify `--user`, `sudo`, `virtualenv` etc. as fits for your setup.

If you want to work off a specific Git version (`HEAD` of the `master` branch by current defaults),
* `git clone` the version of `kubernetes-py` that you are interested in.
* Use `pip3` in [*editable mode*](https://packaging.python.org/tutorials/installing-packages/#installing-from-a-local-src-tree) to install `kubernetes-py` from your local working copy: `pip3 install -e kubernetes-py`. Again, use `--user`, `sudo`, `virtualenv` etc. as desired.

Note that both suggestions differ from my previous approach (where I used `setup.py` directly).

```
import numpy as np

from tensorflow.python import debug as tf_debug

# common part shared by two targets
class CommonPart(snt.AbstractModule):
    def __init__(self, name = "common_part"):
        super(CommonPart, self).__init__(name = name)
        with self._enter_variable_scope():
            self._conv = snt.Conv2D(4, [3, 3])

    def _build(self, inputs):
        return self._conv(inputs)

# one target with specific output 1
class Output1(snt.AbstractModule):
    def __init__(self, output_size, name = "output1"):
        super(Output1, self).__init__(name = name)
        with self._enter_variable_scope():
            self._lin = snt.Linear(output_size)

    def _build(self, inputs):
        return self._lin(inputs)

# another target with specific output 2
class Output2(snt.AbstractModule):
    def __init__(self, output_size, name = "output2"):
        super(Output2, self).__init__(name = name)

# t1 is input for target1

# t2 is input for target 2
t2 = tf.truncated_normal([1, 5, 5, 1])

# l2 is ground truth label for target 2
l2 = tf.constant([[0, 1, 0, 0]])

opt = tf.train.AdamOptimizer(0.01)

# we create the model, the two-head monster
common_part = CommonPart()
output1 = Output1(2)
output2 = Output2(4)

# feed-forward computation
i1 = common_part(t1)
i2 = common_part(t2)

o1 = output1(snt.BatchFlatten()(i1))
o2 = output2(snt.BatchFlatten()(i2))

# losses for separated targets
l1 = tf.nn.softmax_cross_entropy_with_logits(labels = l1, logits = o1)
l2 = tf.nn.softmax_cross_entropy_with_logits(labels = l2, logits = o2)

l = l1 + l2

g1 = [(grad, var) for grad, var in opt.compute_gradients(l1)

g2 = [(grad, var) for grad, var in opt.compute_gradients(l2)

with tf.Session() as sess:

    output = { 'g1': g1_val, 'g2': g2_val, 'g': g_val }

    sio.savemat("output.mat", output)

```

The problem is not possible "occurred while connecting". You probably mean "after connecting" - see the long text below. (Thank you very much for your patch two years ago.)

PUT and DELETE requests can be also safely retried because a retried PUT doesn't change anything and a retried DELETE can't fail since its implementation is equivalent to `DELETE  FROM SObjectType WHERE Id = '...'`.

The repeated POST request is a problem that should be analysed with more arguments: Compared to the default in Requests package, Django is a server framework and a good behavior after long inactivity is more important than for a general tool or a command line utility. The problem is a timeout caused by a broken connection after a long inactivity, where the reset packet has been lost, e.g. because some other layer/device between had been earlier considered the connection inactive. (firewall connection tracking) (More frequent in development than in production.)

A duplicity can be created only if the request header packet passed and ACK received, the body with sObject data has been also passed, but the response header (with return code) or the response body (with the new `Id`) failed. (The ACK for the request body is usually in the response header, it is probably not a special case.) Known broken connections are handled by requests package correctly. It is also not a special case.

The failed request with REQUESTS_MAX_RETRIES=0 would be probably also for you more frequent than the duplicate record with REQUESTS_MAX_RETRIES=1. The question is whether the more frequent is worse, why and when.

A duplicate can be usually easier fixed later than a data loss. What does the user when (s)he receives an error message? Go away or retry all. Even with REQUESTS_MAX_RETRIES=0 you have the same probability that you have by one record more even if think after the exception that nothing has been created. This probability is very low after a long inactivity and guesstimated 50% if the server is somewhat busy.

A small advantage after an exception is that you can know about the uncertainty (inserted or not?), but can not resolve it in the same request.

You can see the previous issue #42 and discussion about the same. I had also reported an issue to Requests and tried a patched Requests and wait long time for their solution before we closed our issue. A useful timeout for request header is useful to be short and the retry is very useful (the fail is very probable and a fast ACK should be expected with some possible protocol level handshake retries and there is no risk of duplicity) The request body should operate also with a short timeout but the retry is by 50% undesirable. The response should operate with a long timeout especially for queries and the retry is completely undesirable, but we need to know the new created ID, otherwise orphans are created. The simply answered that all should be solved by a lower layer, if I remember.

I know a case where the a repeat is really terrible.
Imagine that you have a smart merchandise Order system with an acknowledgement as the second step. You create an order, add order items and finally the order should be confirmed to be complete, otherwise it should be ignored.
- Autorepeat in this step is save and only the last Order could be valid, all duplicates will be automatically ignored.
- Autorepat in OrderItem part is deadly.
- Autorepeat in the acknowledgement part is crucial, since you must definitely know with very high probability whether the Order has been confirmed or not. False positive and false negative would be a big problem.

An important solution for a POST request with retries zero after a longer inactivity is to implement a "ping" request (a fast empty request before). I did not commit it because it seems nobody has had a problem last two years.

It should be documented, but how?  - A complicated description would look like that django-salesforce is a worse then other packages or that the REST API is worse than SOAP API, but the problem is the same and mostly ignored. Every information about it, except a trivial that this setting exists, is invalid by some point of view or so complicated that it is useless.

The root problem is the `last_match()` function, so we could probably just focus on that for now. It should return `None` when there was no match; if it returns `"undefined"` that *could* mean they matched a trigger named `+ undefined` and you wouldn't be able to tell for sure. That's admittedly an edge case (who's going to have a `+ undefined` in their bot?) but should still be taken into account.

I still like having the `default` parameter in the session manager, but then the implementation of `last_match` could be updated to be like this:

```
def last_match(self, username):
    return self._session.get(username, "__lastmatch__", None)
```

So calling `rs.get_uservar()` would have the same behavior wrt undefined variables by returning the string `"undefined"` when the variable doesn't exist, like it does in `<get name>` in RiveScript. This is probably the least surprising behavior for the end user.

In case the developer wants to know whether a variable *actually* is set, a new function could be added like

```
def uservar_exists(self, username, name):
    return self._session.get(username, name, None) != None
```

You don't have to worry about that for this PR, though.

**tl;dr.** suggested changes:

* In `rivescript.sessions` implement the `default` parameter for `get()` as mentioned above.
* In `rivescript.rivescript.last_match()`, have it go directly to the session manager instead of `get_uservar` and send the `default=None` param.
