Hi Victor,

Please, take a look on this page - http://python-dependency-injector.ets-labs.org/containers/index.html. Basically, container - is a group of providers.

> Why not just a class with fields initialized to providers 

`DeclarativeContainer` is just a class which fields are initialized to providers. One of the `DeclarativeContainer` benefits is that it provides also a dictionary of all declared providers:

```python
"""Declarative IoC containers inheritance example."""

import dependency_injector.containers as containers
import dependency_injector.providers as providers


class ContainerA(containers.DeclarativeContainer):
    """Example IoC container A."""

    provider1 = providers.Factory(object)


class ContainerB(ContainerA):
    """Example IoC container B."""

    provider2 = providers.Singleton(object)


# Making some asserts for `providers` attribute:
assert ContainerA.providers == dict(provider1=ContainerA.provider1)
assert ContainerB.providers == dict(provider1=ContainerA.provider1,
                                    provider2=ContainerB.provider2)

# Making some asserts for `cls_providers` attribute:
assert ContainerA.cls_providers == dict(provider1=ContainerA.provider1)
assert ContainerB.cls_providers == dict(provider2=ContainerB.provider2)

# Making some asserts for `inherited_providers` attribute:
assert ContainerA.inherited_providers == dict()
assert ContainerB.inherited_providers == dict(provider1=ContainerB.provider1)
```

This feature is useful when you want to attach some common functionality for a group of providers. Let's consider next example:

```python
class Services(containers.DeclarativeContainer):
    """IoC container of business service providers."""

    users = providers.Factory(example.services.UsersService,
                              db=Gateways.database,
                              logger=Core.logger)

    auth = providers.Factory(example.services.AuthService,
                             db=Gateways.database,
                             logger=Core.logger,
                             token_ttl=Core.config.auth.token_ttl)

    photos = providers.Factory(example.services.PhotosService,
                               db=Gateways.database,
                               s3=Gateways.s3,
                               logger=Core.logger)


# Start all services when application starts:
for service_provider in Services.providers.values():
    service = service_provider()
    service.start()
```

---
Using of dictionaries doesn't work when you need to make injections of providers from the same container:

```python
class MoviesModule(containers.DeclarativeContainer):
    """IoC container of movies module component providers."""

    movie = providers.Factory(movies.models.Movie)
    # ^----------------------------------------------------------------
    finder = providers.AbstractFactory(movies.finders.MovieFinder,  # |
                                       movie_model=movie.provider)  # movie.provider
    # ^--------------------------------------------------------
    lister = providers.Factory(movies.listers.MovieLister,  # |
                               movie_finder=finder)         # finder
```

You also can do things like this:

```python
movies = {}
movies['movie'] = providers.Factory(movies.models.Movie)
movies['finder'] = providers.AbstractFactory(movies.finders.MovieFinder,
                                             movie_model=movies['movie'].provider)
movies['lister'] = providers.Factory(movies.listers.MovieLister,
                                     movie_finder=movies['finder'])
```

or you can use `DynamicContainer` - http://python-dependency-injector.ets-labs.org/containers/dynamic.html

Hope it gives more clarity.

Thanks,

@musiKk I slightly disagree here:

* I assume you have a CD pipeline (right?), so your process is automated and makes sure that it does the right thing
* when using Senza manually from the CLI, it's often about deleting test stacks or similar --- I don't want to  specify an additional option only to convince the tool to do the right think (the option quickly would become something like `rm -fr` where `-fr` is so common that it's always there, so the "safety" feature is gone)
* whether a stack receives traffic is not trivial to find out as the DNS record weights will not tell you whether actual traffic goes to the stack, i.e. removing traffic via DNS from a stack and immediately deleting the stack would still cause havoc due to DNS caching (TTL) and client connection pools 
* there are also other reasons why a stack might not be safe to delete, just think about long running computation processes or jobs or other "stateful" stacks or configuration templates (e.g. creating a Senza template for security groups)

Below is an implementation of adding noise to the gradient based on Adding
Gradient Noise Improves Learning for Very Deep Networks. Adding gradient
noise can also improve curriculum learning!

Starter code is based on Jan Schlueter's code for Saiency Maps and Guided
Backpropagation
https://github.com/Lasagne/Recipes/blob/master/examples/Saliency Maps
and Guided
Backpropagation.ipynb

class ModifiedBackprop(object):
    def __init__(self, nonlinearity, epoch, prev_seq_err):
        self.nonlinearity = nonlinearity
        self.epoch = epoch
        self.prev_seq_err = prev_seq_err
        self.rnd_normal = None
        self.ops = {}  # memoizes an OpFromGraph instance per tensor type

    def __call__(self, x):
        # OpFromGraph is oblique to Theano optimizations, so we need to move
        # things to GPU ourselves if needed.
        if theano.sandbox.cuda.cuda_enabled:
            maybe_to_gpu = theano.sandbox.cuda.as_cuda_ndarray_variable
        else:
            maybe_to_gpu = lambda x: x
        # We move the input to GPU if needed.
        x = maybe_to_gpu(x)
        # We note the tensor type of the input variable to the nonlinearity
        # (mainly dimensionality and dtype); we need to create a fitting Op.
        tensor_type = x.type
        # If we did not create a suitable Op yet, this is the time to do so.
        if tensor_type not in self.ops:
            # For the graph, we create an input variable of the correct
type:
            inp = tensor_type()
            # We pass it through the nonlinearity (and move to GPU if
needed).
            outp = maybe_to_gpu(self.nonlinearity(inp))
            # Then we fix the forward expression...
            op = theano.OpFromGraph([inp], [outp])
            # ...and replace the gradient with our own (defined in a
subclass).
            op.grad = self.grad
            # Finally, we memoize the new Op
            self.ops[tensor_type] = op
        # And apply the memoized Op to the input we got.
        return self.ops[tensor_type](x)


class GuidedBackProp(ModifiedBackprop):
    def grad(self, inputs, out_grads):
        (inp, ) = inputs
        (grd, ) = out_grads
        dtype = inp.dtype
        if self.rnd_normal is None:
            srng = RandomStreams(seed=234)
            rnd_normal = srng.normal(T.shape(grd))
        prev_seq_err = 1.
        noise_param = T.sqrt(T.pow(1+self.epoch, -0.55) * prev_seq_err)
        noise_scale = 0.0
        noise = rnd_normal * noise_param * noise_scale
        return (grd.astype(dtype) + noise, )


epoch_shared = theano.shared(1)
prev_seq_err_shared = theano.shared(1)


Substitute your linearities with

    GuidedBackProp(lasagne.nonlinearities.sigmoid, epoch_shared,
prev_seq_err_shared)

HTH

The notion of a "global variable" is very sketchy on the binary level, and there's no easy way to just make _all_ of them symbolic without seriously messing up the rest of the program, e.g. making the pointer to stdout symbolic.

For using DIRAC grid proxy tools I also need to set the following config options:
```ini
[defaults_GridCommand]
init = dirac-proxy-init -g gridpp_user -M -t
info = dirac-proxy-info
destroy = dirac-proxy-info
```

**Note** however that for me at least in a vanilla environment this was *not* sufficient. Ganga would complain that there was no such command as `dirac-proxy-xxx` (i.e. no correct Dirac UI in the calling process).

After consultation with @rob-c it seems that his solution is to modify the above to the following:
```ini
[defaults_GridCommand]
init = source /home/hep/arichard/dirac_ui/bashrc && dirac-proxy-init -g gridpp_user -M -t
info = source /home/hep/arichard/dirac_ui/bashrc && dirac-proxy-info
destroy = source /home/hep/arichard/dirac_ui/bashrc && dirac-proxy-info
```

After doing this things do indeed now work fine.

---

This however is *very* convoluted and I don't know how users are expected to be able to do this. What's more this seems pointless as we already give Ganga the path to the Dirac bashrc in the DIRAC config section:

```ini
[DIRAC]
DiracEnvSource = /home/hep/arichard/dirac_ui/bashrc
```
Is it worth perhaps looking at picking this up automatically when running the grid commands or should we just wait till @milliams credentials patch nukes all this? At the very least I've found it useful to look into and document it in this issue such that if anyone comes across it in the future we know what to do.

Let me put my previous comment in perspective: it wasn't a threat, and I'm not sure a fork is necessarily the best solution.

This is FOSS, kindly written by @f1nality , and it's very useful - so much so that we're all here clamouring for more. I didn't mean to imply that he was doing a bad job, nor that he had any obligation to address our needs, and I'm sorry if I came across that way.

That said, there's clearly the need for faster development. A fork is always a possibility, but it's a somehow drastic one. Besides, who's to say that the fork will then keep on being developed? I'm sure @f1nality didn't plan on a hiatus, when he started this. Nobody is exempt from starting/picking up a project with the best intentions and then having to step away for a while, or drop it completely.

So, my suggestion to the people willing to take matters into their own hands is: reach out to @f1nality , and see if you can join as co-admins of the project. This immediately means more hands and eyes on the project, more PR reviewers, and hopefully a faster pace. It also means less fragmentation, and the continuation of what's after all a very nice project.

I'm in now way volunteering to do any of this - I have far too little time in my hands, and I've already forked django-jet to apply this fix. But I've seen way too many well-intentioned forks that end up as dead as the original project, and I hope we can avoid this here.

Best of luck!

When Google's codebase was initially envisioned, a "small" test was defined along the lines of basically being a unit test that tests a single class, mocks out its dependencies, mocks out the file system, and mocks out clocks. It's not supposed to do i/o, call sleep, etc. Anything that does is considered medium.

Please keep in mind that these definitions were created by C++ and Java programmers. It's more dubious how they're supposed to be applied to scripting languages. But for the time being, keeping the deadlines happy is an improvement.

We originally had `otPlatFlash*` APIs as you are suggesting here.  OpenThread switched to the current model with #889.  The argument was that use of `otPlatFlash*` APIs were implementation and platform specific, whereas OpenThread platform APIs are intended to be implementation and platform agnostic.  In this case, the `settings.cpp` file was an implementation of `otPlatSettings*` that flash-based systems may choose to use.  However, `settings.cpp` would not apply to non-flash-based systems and flash-based systems may choose to implement `otPlatSettings*` in a different way.

With that background, happy to revisit and discuss this decision again.  @wbober, @pvanhorn, and any others please share any additional thoughts you may have.

Because xmltodict will translate:

```
<interfaces>
   <interface>
        <name>et1</name>
   </interface>
</interfaces>
```

to:

```
{"interfaces" {"interfaces": {"name": "et1"}}}
```

while:

```
<interfaces>
   <interface>
        <name>et1</name>
   </interface>
   <interface>
        <name>et2</name>
   </interface>
</interfaces>
```

to:
```
{"interfaces" {"interfaces": [
   {"name": "et1"},
   {"name": "et2"},]
}}
```

XML doesn't have the notion of lists or dicts. Everything is a container and containers can be repeated which means `xmltodict` can't know if a container with one element is a python dict or a python list.

That's why you need this argument:

https://github.com/martinblech/xmltodict/blob/master/xmltodict.py#L258

So I am sure this is going to be a constant source of problems where it works in the CI because we have two or more elements of something but as soon as a user has only one element everything breaks. In all honesty, working with XML should be easy enough to avoid unnecessary risks.

Apologies in advance for the following rant: I think that repeatedly bringing xtensor into the discussions here at pybind11 is not conductive. If there is some clever technical insight that can be ported to this project, then that's all fine with me. However, there is really no reason why the API/style of this project should somehow be bound to that of xtensor (rather than NumPy, which we're trying to wrap)

(From the original issue)

*I agree that when exposing a feature to Python, it must be written in such a way that regardless of the values passed to it, it cannot crash the interpreter, but these accessors are not directly exposed to the Python users. Instead, I think that one of the main reasons people would right c++ extensions with pybind is because they want speed, and they might be disappointed with this behavior. #500 already improved the situation, but as we discussed there, I would prefer to completely remove the bound checking.*

There's no way to hide it as far as I know. Pydoc is [hardcoded](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/6f0eb93183519024cb360162bdd81b9faec97ba6/Lib/pydoc.py#L1319) to skip over `builtins.object` bases, so we can't opt into the same behavior.

This is a fairly significant design change, and I'm wondering if it may end up complicating the library internals (e.g. the difference between ``handle`` and ``object`` is already very confusing those who haven't used the Python C API. This adds yet another type.).

Being able to ``release()`` an ``object`` or move is very useful to interface with the C API. The ability to std::move() objects helps to prevent some useless inc/dec-refs (leaner codegen). How would this work with the proposed approach?

The alternative and less intrusive breaking change would be to do a pass over the API to ensure that functions like ``cast()`` and ``handle::operator[]`` and ``handle::attr()`` throw ``error_already_set`` rather than returning null pointers.

> Is where any way to make lz4 return str, as it did before 0.12?

Not at present. I have to admit, I went back and forwards on this a bit. Let me explain the rationale.

Consider the use case where the user calls `lz4.frame.decompress(data)` where `data` contains a frame (or part of a frame) of compressed data, ending with the end of frame marker. After the end of frame, `data` could contain more data. That could either be another LZ4 frame, or just other useful data for the application. When `lz4.frame.decompress(data)` returns, it will only have consumed data up until the end of the frame, and the user will be interested to know where to begin reading the extra data from. The returned number of bytes (`bytes_read`) facilitates the user doing something like `extra_data=data[bytes_read:]`. Without returning the `bytes_read`, the user wouldn't readily know where the frame had ended. I have had reports in the past of people wanting to use `lz4.frame.decompress` with data like this (indeed the origins of this functionality come from a pull request).

Ok, so that's the use case, and I implemented the simplest thing possible, requiring the least memory consumption (an alternative would have been to return a new string with the unused data). However, I am not married to this, necessarily.

A different solution would be to have a boolean option for `lz4.frame.decompress`, say `return_bytes_read`, which defaults to `False`: 
* If `return_bytes_read` is `False` then `lz4.frame.decompress` returns only the uncompressed data.
* If `return_bytes_read` is `True` then `lz4.frame.decompress` returns the uncompressed data and `bytes_read` as a tuple.

Would welcome your thoughts on that, and alternative suggestions.

> If the None values are not there then there are not the same number of columns at each insertion.

That is a very good point and certainly something to fix, but that does not vote neither for nor against having users explicitly specifying `None` values.

I guess my only concern is that something like
```
meas = Measurement()
meas.register_parameter(x)
meas.register_parameter(y)
meas.register_parameter(z)

with meas.run() as datasaver: 
   datasaver.add_result((x, 0))
   datasaver.add_result((x, 0), (y, None))
   datasaver.add_result((x, 0), (z, None))
   datasaver.add_result((x, 0), (y, None), (z, None))
```
where the four last lines do the same thing is perhaps (?) a potential source of confusion.

I suppose there are three possible ways to go:

1. Maintaining that `None` values can only be added implicitly by their omission.
2. Allowing for both omitting a value and explicitly declaring that it is `None`.
3. Only allowing explicitly passing `None` values. 

Both 1. and 2. would require some extra work to fix the column mismatch bug that Sohail pointed out. I'd vote for either 1. or 2.

> to seek through Twitch VODs

Seeking does only work if the player is able to request a certain byte range of the video file.

Since Twitch is using HLS, you can simply use the `--player-passthrough hls` parameter, which will pass Twitch's stream HLS link to your player which will then read the stream data on its own. This way it is able to request the correct byte range if you're jumping to a different position in the VOD. Your player needs to support HLS, though.

I don't know if it's possible to implement this behavior into streamlink if a different passthrough method or stdin is being used as player input. If it is, I would very much welcome such an addition, but I doubt that it's possible.

Jep starts the underlying Python interpreter as the Python main thread, then isolates that away from API so no one can use it.  We did this because doing Python actions on the main thread can lead to GIL deadlock, so it's a safety feature.  At present there is no way to get that main thread that started the interpreter.  [Python documentation says that signal must be run on the main thread](https://docs.python.org/3/library/signal.html#signal.signal).

Originally, OptimizedRNN was a motivation for optional inputs. With the splitting of OptimizedRNN into multiple separate ops, do we still have a need for optional inputs? Do we currently have a need for input_arg_count? I am just trying to understand if this discussion is for future extension or we need it right now.

One concern I have is the following: even though the ONNX standard is supposed to be a specification for only the serialized representation of the model, the design choice here seems to be partly motivated by the underlying (current) implementation of operators, especially in terms of how input parameters are passed in to the operator implementation. 

Since ONNX-ML also makes use of the richer "sequence" type (for input values), would we then support both? The richer sequence can have length that is dynamically determined and is represented as a single input of sequence type; while the above-discussed one has a length that is statically known and is represented as a "variadic" input.  Do we need to support both?

For sockets, removing cimport message resolves the issue perfectly.

For message.pxd and anything else depending on libzmq, the problem is that
when you try to compile the .c file of your external library, you get
unresolved references to "allocate.h" and "zmq_compat.h", which look to be
part of the pyzmq internal implementation. I think that if those references
were moved to a separate .pxd file and only referenced from the libzmq .pyx
implementation, the problem would be resolved for anything referencing
libzmq. References to zmq.h should be fine since any user will have it
installed.
